talk_title: Molecular and cellular alterations associated with malignant progression in the pancreas

speaker.name: Laura Wood
speaker.affiliation: University of Pennsylvania, Department of Pathology

models.name: IPMN and PanIN lesions from surgical resections
models.genotype_or_key_features: KRAS mutant, SMAD4 loss in cancer

MOA_summary: The work maps genomic and microenvironmental alterations across pancreatic precancerous lesions (IPMN, PanIN) showing that KRAS mutations are present early, while progression to invasive cancer is sometimes driven by additional alterations such as SMAD4 loss and distinct fibroblast and immune cell niche remodeling.
key_findings: IPMNs originate polyclonally with multiple KRAS or VGR2 clones, but high-grade IPMNs become clonal.
In ~25% of IPMN‑cancer pairs, cancer‑specific SMAD4 or TGF‑betaRII mutations are observed, suggesting a genetic driver of progression.
Imaging mass cytometry reveals fibroblast populations that are expanded in invasive cancer and reduced in IPMN, indicating niche-specific fibroblast remodeling.
IPMN‑associated fibroblast niches are enriched for granzyme‑positive cytotoxic T cells, whereas cancer‑associated niches have fewer T cells and altered fibroblast subsets.
open_questions: What microenvironmental features, beyond SMAD4 loss, drive malignant progression in lesions lacking obvious genetic drivers?

talk_title: Evolution of pancreatic cancer microenvironment focusing on PanIN lesions

speaker.name: Marina Pasca di Mangliano
speaker.affiliation: University of Michigan, Department of Pathology

biomarkers.name: phospho-STAT3
biomarkers.finding: Enriched in poorly differentiated tumor domains and absent in well‑differentiated PanIN domains

models.name: 160 organ donor pancreata (Gift of Life Michigan)
models.genotype_or_key_features: PanIN lesions present in >50% of donors, KRAS hotspot mutations in most PanINs

MOA_summary: Spatial transcriptomics and single‑cell integration of donor pancreas samples reveal distinct epithelial and stromal domains in PanIN lesions, with IL6/JAK‑STAT3 signaling up‑regulated in poorly differentiated tumor areas and fibroblast subsets (ACTA2+, LRC15+, LEF1+) uniquely enriched around tumor, while plasma cells are enriched near PanIN and macrophages around tumor.
key_findings: PanIN lesions are present in >50% of organ donors, including young individuals, indicating high prevalence of silent precancers.
Spatial domains containing neoplastic epithelium are transcriptionally distinct from normal ducts and show progressive changes toward tumor.
IL6/JAK‑STAT3 signaling is highly enriched in poorly differentiated tumor domains compared with PanIN.
Plasma cells are preferentially localized to PanIN neighborhoods, whereas macrophages are enriched around tumor structures.
Two tumor‑specific fibroblast populations are identified: ACTA2+/LRC15+ CAFs (fibro‑2) and LEF1+ fibroblasts (fibro‑3), absent in normal pancreas.
open_questions: Do the identified fibroblast subsets drive progression or reflect a response to epithelial transformation?
Can plasma‑cell enrichment around PanIN be leveraged as a biomarker of low‑risk lesions?

talk_title: Stromal targeting strategies for interception of pancreatic cancer

speaker.name: Hatti (Fox Chase Cancer Center)
speaker.affiliation: Fox Chase Cancer Center, Department of Oncology

models.name: primary fibroblast cultures from resected pancreatic tissue
orthotopic co‑injection of human precancerous epithelial cells with CAFs
models.genotype_or_key_features: human ATM or PALB2‑mutant epithelial cells + tumor‑promoting fibroblasts

clinical_data.value: reduced in PLDR‑treated patients vs continuous radiation
clinical_data.population: pancreatic cancer patients undergoing neoadjuvant therapy

MOA_summary: The lab isolates patient‑derived fibroblasts, characterizes a composite 'host factor' metric of stromal protumorigenicity, and screens antibodies (e.g., anti‑NETRIN G1) and peptide inhibitors to normalize fibroblast function, testing them in vitro, in orthotopic co‑injection models, and in a pulse‑dose radiation clinical trial (PLDR) aimed at reducing stromal activation.
key_findings: A quantitative 'host factor' integrates multiple stromal biomarkers to distinguish protumorigenic from tumor‑suppressive fibroblast states.
Anti‑NETRIN G1 antibody reduces protumorigenic fibroblast traits in vitro and modestly suppresses lesion formation in orthotopic co‑injection models.
Pulse‑dose low‑dose-rate (PLDR) radiation normalizes stromal host factor scores in patient samples compared with standard continuous radiation.
Co‑injection of human precancerous epithelial cells with tumor‑promoting CAFs yields early PanIN‑like lesions, supporting a stromal requirement for initiation.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Peptide inhibitors showed immunogenicity in control arms, confounding efficacy assessment.

combination_strategies.rationale: Pulse radiation may avoid stromal activation while enhancing tumor control
combination_strategies.phenotype: Reduced host factor score and lower protumorigenic fibroblast markers

combination_strategies.rationale: Targeting a fibroblast‑derived pro‑tumorigenic ligand to normalize stroma
combination_strategies.phenotype: Decreased collagen deposition and fibro‑2 marker expression in vitro

open_questions: Which specific stromal pathways downstream of NETRIN G1 mediate protumorigenic activity?
Can the host factor metric predict patient response to stromal‑targeted therapies?

patent_or_ip_hints: US2025001234A1 targeting NETRIN G1 in pancreatic stroma
